content.full_text:
This memorandum, dated April 15, 1977, is from the Fair Political Practices Commission and is addressed to File. The subject of the memo is a telephone conversation with Michael Eddy on March 15, 1977.

Michael Eddy is a former law partner of, and the attorney for, Judge Michael Grier. Judge Grier was appointed to the San Diego County Superior Court on February 28, 1977. Although the Judge understands that on his first Statement of Economic Interest he will not have to disclose any income, he and his former partners are in the process of developing a buy-out agreement and are interested in how the form of that agreement might affect Judge Grier's obligation to disclose income on his first anniversary statement in 1978.

The arrangement that they have apparently settled on is that Judge Grier has no right to receive any of the accounts payable as of the date he terminates his relationship with the partnership (February 25, 1977). However, Judge Grier will be entitled to receive 12 percent of all fees realized from contingency fee matters with the firm as of his date of termination and 10 percent of all workmen's compensation case fees in the office as of the date of his termination. There is no termination date on the agreement. Therefore, Judge Grier can receive a share of these fees whenever they are realized. If none of these cases produce fees, Judge Grier is entitled to no compensation from his firm.

I advised Mr. Eddy that under this agreement, any clients from which Judge Grier receives fees, if his pro rata share is $250 or more, would be disclosable on Schedule C of Form 721. I said that his source of income would be the clients, not the firm since his right to receive fees is tied directly to the fortunes of a particular client.

MB:jo

MB:jo

sections.question_synthetic: How should Judge Michael Grier disclose income from his former law partnership on his Statement of Economic Interest (Form 721), specifically regarding fees from contingency and workers' compensation cases earned after his judicial appointment?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Under the buy-out agreement where Judge Grier receives a percentage of contingency and workers' compensation case fees realized after his termination date, any clients from which he receives $250 or more in pro rata fees must be disclosed on Schedule C of Form 721, with the disclosure source being the individual clients rather than the law firm.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal FPPC memorandum documenting telephone advice; no formal Q&A sections present; question and conclusion synthesized from narrative description of the telephone conversation and FPPC's advice; initials 'MB:jo' at end suggest internal authorship/typing notation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should Judge Michael Grier disclose income from his former law partnership on his Statement of Economic Interest (Form 721), specifically regarding fees from contingency and workers' compensation cases earned after his judicial appointment?

CONCLUSION: Under the buy-out agreement where Judge Grier receives a percentage of contingency and workers' compensation case fees realized after his termination date, any clients from which he receives $250 or more in pro rata fees must be disclosed on Schedule C of Form 721, with the disclosure source being the individual clients rather than the law firm.

There is no termination date on the agreement. Therefore, Judge Grier can receive a share of these fees whenever they are realized. embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum advising that Judge Michael Grier, recently appointed to San Diego County Superior Court, must disclose income from his former law partnership's contingency and workers' compensation fees on his Statement of Economic Interest if his pro rata share from any client equals or exceeds $250, listing clients rather than the firm as the income source.
